mainEntity.name: What mode should I use to photograph fireworks?

acceptedAnswer.text: Manual mode is the safest choice, ideally with bulb mode. Fireworks change brightness constantly, so relying on automatic exposure usually gives inconsistent results.

mainEntity.name: What aperture is best for fireworks photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: A good starting point is around f/8. If the display is sparse, you may move towards f/9 or f/10. If the finale becomes dense and bright, f/6.3 to f/8 can be more practical.

mainEntity.name: What ISO should I use for fireworks?

acceptedAnswer.text: Keep ISO low. ISO 100 on full frame and ISO 200 on crop is a strong starting point. Low ISO helps keep the sky clean and the colours stronger.

mainEntity.name: Do I need a tripod for fireworks photography?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, for the classic long-exposure fireworks look a tripod is essential. It gives you stability, sharper trails and proper control over bulb exposures.

mainEntity.name: Should I use autofocus for fireworks?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Pre-focus before the display starts, then switch to manual focus. Autofocus often hunts in the dark and causes missed shots.

mainEntity.name: Why do my fireworks photos look smoky and dull later in the display?

acceptedAnswer.text: Because smoke builds up as the display continues. It can soften contrast, flatten colour and make the sky look muddy. Earlier frames are often cleaner.
You can, but it gives a different result. Handheld shooting is more useful for quick documentary-style images rather than classic flowing light trails.

mainEntity.name: What lens is best for photographing fireworks?

acceptedAnswer.text: A wide-angle lens is often the best starting point, especially if you want foreground and a sense of place. It is usually better to frame slightly wider and crop later than clip the top of the burst.
